joke.raw_joke: I got thrown out of my local park after arranging the squirrels by height. — They didn't like me critter sizing

blocks.content:
### seed-188

**MILO_OFFICIAL_SCRIPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-188
edit_level: polish

script:
- setup: "I got thrown out of my local park for arranging the squirrels by height."
- punchline: "Turns out nobody likes critter sizing."
- tag: ~

changes_made: Changed "after arranging" to "for arranging" — tighter causal link. Added "Turns out" to open the punchline — it frames the line as a discovered universal truth, which is funnier than a personal grievance. Changed "didn't like me critter sizing" to "nobody likes critter sizing" — the generalization is better; this is apparently a park-wide policy, maybe a broader social consensus, and the narrator is just finding out.

caption_notes: Two cards. Setup: "I got thrown out of my local park for arranging the squirrels by height." Punchline: "Turns out nobody likes critter sizing." — "critter sizing" should ideally be bolded or highlighted on the punchline card to give the viewer a visual beat to parse "criticizing" underneath it.

timing_notes: Setup delivered with mild aggrievement — he was ejected for something that seemed reasonable. 0.4s pause. Punchline at normal pace with slight emphasis on "critter" — it needs to hit clearly enough that the listener catches "criticizing" a half-second after landing. Do not rush the final word. Hold it just slightly.

tts_notes: "Critter sizing" — KRIT-er SY-zing. The second syllable of "sizing" needs stress: SY, not siz. If the syllable stress is wrong the wordplay gets muddy. Phonetic fallback: "KRIT-er SY-zing."

quality_flag: solid

---

### seed-188

**RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1**

**Primary Cast:**

| Role | Cast | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| Narrator (ejected from park, squirrel height incident) | Milo Quip — Snarky Student persona (adapted: Teen Who Did This and Sees No Problem With It) | The joke requires a narrator who arranged squirrels by height and will describe the ejection as the unreasonable part of the story. Milo's "youth skeptic / punchline sniper" archetype delivers the absurdist premise with complete deadpan sincerity — he would do this, it made sense at the time, and he is mildly aggrieved to have been ejected for it. The indignation is real. He stands by the activity. |

**Adaptation Notes:**
- Milo: Casual teen wardrobe. Post-ejection energy — slightly disheveled, maybe a leaf on his jacket. Could be sitting just outside the park gate or on a bench nearby. No props required, but a park-adjacent setting (bench, grass, fence line) establishes the scene immediately.
- Visual background option for setup shot: 2-3 squirrels arranged in ascending height visible in background illustration — small, medium, large, spaced evenly. They should look deliberate. The arrangement is tidy. This is the work.

**Shot Intent:**
- **Setup:** Milo to camera, describing the incident with mild affront. His body language is still slightly in the "arranging" posture — arms at different heights, maybe. He was doing something organized. He was interrupted.
- **Punchline:** Hold on Milo. "Turns out nobody likes critter sizing." He delivers this as a universal law of nature he has only just discovered. His expression does not change after the punchline. He still doesn't see the problem. He might do it again.

---

### seed-188

**IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1**

seed_id: seed-188
concepts_count: 2

Milo script: ✅ Received. Setup: "I got thrown out of my local park for arranging the squirrels by height." Punchline: "Turns out nobody likes critter sizing." "Turns out" frames it as discovered universal truth. "Critter sizing" ideally bolded on punchline card.
Riley casting honored: Milo Quip (Snarky Student) solo. Post-ejection energy — disheveled, leaf on jacket. Optional squirrel height lineup in background.

---

**Concept A — "Just Outside the Gate"**
Milo sitting on a low wall just outside a park entrance. Slightly disheveled. The squirrels are still in formation behind him through the fence. His work survived his ejection.
Duration: 8.4s — Tight solo delivery. The squirrel lineup in the background is the visual joke; the verbal pun is the second joke.

Master video prompt:
"Just outside a park entrance, cartoon illustration 2.5D, clean bold lines, warm afternoon light, vertical 9:16 portrait. Milo Quip (teen boy, spiky tousled brown hair, simple features, dark charcoal t-shirt, denim jacket slightly askew on shoulders, small brown leaf clinging to one sleeve) sitting on a low stone wall beside a wrought-iron park gate. A sign reads 'OAKFIELD PARK.' Through the gate/fence, on the green grass inside the park, three cartoon squirrels sit in a tidy row arranged by ascending height — small, medium, large — spaced evenly. Warm afternoon sunlight, trees in background. Caption-safe lower 20% clear."

Scene 1 (0.0–1.8s): HOOK
A wrought-iron park gate with a sign reading "OAKFIELD PARK" and a smaller notice underneath (park rules). The gate is closed. Just outside the gate, sitting on a low stone wall to the right of the entrance, is Milo Quip — a teenage boy with spiky tousled brown hair, simple features, wearing a dark charcoal t-shirt under a denim jacket. The jacket is askew on his shoulders — one sleeve half-slipped, as if he was guided out without violence but without ceremony. A small brown leaf clings to his left sleeve. He does not know it is there. His expression is mild grievance — the face of someone who has been wrongly ejected and is being very composed about it. Through the park gate behind him, on the soft green grass in warm afternoon sunlight, three cartoon squirrels are visible — sitting in a tidy row, arranged by ascending height: a small squirrel on the left, a medium squirrel in the center, a large squirrel on the right. They are evenly spaced. The arrangement is precise, deliberate, organized. This is the work. The work is still there. Milo is not.

Scene 2 (1.8–4.6s): SETUP
Same park entrance exterior. Milo Quip on the stone wall (spiky brown hair, charcoal t-shirt, askew denim jacket, leaf on sleeve) faces the viewer. His expression is mild aggrievement, not humiliation — a person filing a report they disagree with. One hand gestures behind him toward the park gate — toward the squirrels still arranged on the grass. His other hand rests on his knee. His body still carries the energy of the arranging activity — arms at slightly different heights, the unconscious posture of someone who was recently sorting things by size. Through the gate, the three squirrels remain in their ascending row — small, medium, large — undisturbed. They did not scatter after Milo left. They are holding formation. Warm afternoon light filters through the trees.
Caption: "I got thrown out of my local park for arranging the squirrels by height."

Scene 3 (4.6–4.9s): BEAT
Closer on Milo. His expression does not shift toward a grin or a smirk. It stays level — the calm surface of someone who has processed a rejection and arrived at a conclusion about it. He is about to share what he learned.

Scene 4 (4.9–8.4s): PUNCHLINE
Same park entrance. Milo Quip on the stone wall (spiky hair, charcoal t-shirt, denim jacket, leaf on sleeve) looks at the viewer with flat sincerity. He delivers the line as a discovered universal truth — something he now knows about the world that he did not know before. "Critter sizing" is, to him, the accurate name for the activity he was performing. He arranged critters by size. That is critter sizing. People objected. He has noted the objection. He does not agree with it, but he has accepted that it exists. His expression after the line does not change — no reaction to his own pun, because there is no pun from his perspective. There is an activity and a consequence. Through the gate behind him, the squirrels hold their line — small, medium, large — a monument to work that was not appreciated. The leaf clings to his sleeve.
Caption: "Turns out nobody likes critter sizing."

Caption plan: 0.0–1.8s silent | 1.8–4.6s setup | 4.9–8.4s punchline. Bold or highlight "critter sizing" on punchline card per Milo's caption note.
Voice: Milo mildly aggrieved, recounting. 0.4s pause. Punchline at normal pace with slight natural emphasis on "critter" — KRIT-er SY-zing. Hold the final word. No rush. The audience needs a half-beat after "sizing" to map it to "criticizing."
TTS note: "Critter sizing" = KRIT-er SY-zing. Second syllable of "sizing" needs stress: SY, not siz. Confirm pronunciation.
Risk: LOW. Single character, park exterior. Squirrels in an ascending row may render imprecisely in AI gen, but cartoon style forgives — three blob-shaped animals of different sizes is achievable. Fallback: Remove squirrels entirely. Just Milo outside a park gate, disheveled, telling the story. The lineup is verbal-only.
Jerry hook: "The squirrels are still in formation after Milo got thrown out. His work survived his ejection. That background detail rewards anyone who looks past the main character — and the leaf on his sleeve that he doesn't notice is a free bonus gag for attentive viewers."
Vince handoff: aspectRatio 9:16 | durationSec 8.4 | sceneCount 4 | mustKeep [Scene 1: hook — disheveled Milo + squirrel lineup visible through gate, Scene 4: punchline delivery + squirrels still in row] | trims [Scene 3 can cut to 0.15s]

---

**Concept B — "The Evidence"**
Milo holds his phone toward the camera. On the screen: a photo of the squirrel arrangement. He documented his work before they threw him out. He came prepared.
Duration: 8.8s — The phone-as-evidence adds a visual layer and a character detail.

Master video prompt:
"Plain warm background or park-adjacent sidewalk, cartoon illustration 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16 portrait. Milo Quip (teen, spiky tousled brown hair, charcoal t-shirt, denim jacket askew, leaf on sleeve) standing and holding a smartphone toward the viewer, screen facing out. On the phone screen: a photograph of a green park lawn with three cartoon squirrels arranged in a perfect ascending line — small, medium, large. Warm natural light. Caption-safe lower 20% clear."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK
A park-adjacent sidewalk — a strip of concrete with grass on one side, a bench visible in the background. Warm afternoon light. Milo Quip stands centered in the frame — spiky tousled brown hair, charcoal t-shirt, denim jacket slightly askew on his shoulders, a small brown leaf on his left sleeve. He holds a smartphone toward the viewer, screen facing out. On the phone screen: a photograph of a green park lawn. In the photo, three cartoon squirrels sit in a precise ascending row — a small one on the left, a medium one in the center, a large one on the right. They are evenly spaced and clearly arranged by height. The photo is well-composed. Milo took this picture before they threw him out. He documented his achievement. His expression behind the phone is quiet pride — the restrained satisfaction of an artist presenting their portfolio piece.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.0s): SETUP
Same location. Milo Quip (spiky hair, charcoal t-shirt, denim jacket, leaf) has lowered the phone slightly so his full face is visible. The phone screen still shows the squirrel lineup photograph. He faces the viewer, recounting what happened. His expression is mild grievance layered over lingering pride — he was doing good work and was punished for it, and here is the proof that the work was good. One hand holds the phone (evidence). The other makes a small "can you believe this" open-palm gesture. He is not sorry. The photo proves he shouldn't be.
Caption: "I got thrown out of my local park for arranging the squirrels by height."

Scene 3 (5.0–5.3s): BEAT
Closer on Milo. He lowers the phone to his side. Evidence presented, case submitted. His expression levels to flat — the discovery incoming.

Scene 4 (5.3–8.8s): PUNCHLINE
Same location. Milo Quip (spiky hair, charcoal t-shirt, denim jacket) looks at the viewer. The phone is at his side — evidence delivered, context established. His expression is flat sincerity — the universal truth he discovered. "Critter sizing" is what he was doing. It has a name. The name describes the activity accurately: sizing critters. People objected. He has accepted that people object. He has not accepted that they are correct. His face does not react to his own line. The leaf remains on his sleeve. He does not see it. It may be there forever.
Caption: "Turns out nobody likes critter sizing."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s silent | 2.0–5.0s setup | 5.3–8.8s punchline. Bold "critter sizing."
Voice: Same Milo delivery. 0.4s pause. Same emphasis notes.
Risk: LOW. Phone screen detail may be small — the squirrel lineup needs to be simple and readable (three shapes, ascending sizes, green background). Fallback: Skip the phone. Milo tells the story to camera, holds up three fingers at different heights to indicate the squirrel arrangement.
Jerry hook: "Milo took a PHOTO before they escorted him out. He documented the work. That one detail tells you everything about who this person is — the kind of teen who arranges squirrels by height and keeps receipts. The phone adds a layer Concept A doesn't have."
Vince handoff: aspectRatio 9:16 | durationSec 8.8 | sceneCount 4 | mustKeep [Scene 1: phone with squirrel photo + Milo's pride, Scene 4: punchline delivery] | trims [Scene 3 can cut to 0.15s]

---

quality_gate: PASS — Two meaningfully different concepts (background squirrels through gate vs. phone evidence), full timestamps, punchline readable, vertical framing preserved, fallbacks provided.
status: ready_for_jerry_review
